So let's talk due diligence. I mentioned in your LOI that your due diligence period would be 21 to 30 days. This is that initial evaluation of the property itself, the rent roll to the roof, should I move forward. It's critical to success as a real estate investor that you do proper due diligence. You need to do your DD on every single deal, every single time on every single unit. And if you're not going to do it, if you don't have the expertise, if you don't know how to evaluate things like cabinets, floors, carpets, the cost to replace to do a proper assessment and audit of the rent roll, the leases. If you don't know how to do those things, I'm not saying you're not ready, but I'm saying you need to get somebody that is ready to do that due diligence. Typically we'll pay $25 to $40 for my management company to actually do the walkthrough of the property and give us their assessment of their due diligence. It's good to have a third party. That way you're not too much of a pessimist nor an optimist, but you get the third party evaluation of the property. Look, if you don't do the due diligence, you could wind up walking into a bad deal or worse, you could end up walking on a deal that was unbelievable. People tell me all the time, I'm worried about getting in a bad deal. What you ought to be worried about is not pulling the trigger on the deal of your damn life. Many real estate investors gloss over this step or don't start the real estate game because they're overwhelmed by cabinets, floors, roughs, clubhouses, rent rolls, audits. Look, hire somebody. We're all idiots about something at some point in our lives. Don't be an idiot and not know it. Sometimes the due diligence in the due diligence is not clear what constitutes a proper due diligence. In other times, people are just plain scared. What does due diligence mean? Due diligence, proper homework, investigate, trust, but verify. Sounds like a big deal because it is, but it's not that big a deal. And in many cases, it's enough to scare off the other buyers. And that is the problem and the opportunity. In this module, we're going to break down the due diligence process that my management team uses. I'm going to provide you with a simple checklist as your anchor by working from this strategic jumping off point, whether you decide to hire somebody else to do it or assist you in doing it or do it yourself, you'll be able to master the due diligence process fast and ensure that you're always walking into the right deal at the right market at the right time so you can pull the trigger and make money. You want to buy deals that make sense for your business right now. Now that being said, you need to know before you do the due diligence whether or not you're doing this deal. This is a tricky thing. If you enter into an LOI that the seller agrees to, then you walk into your due diligence and find out something in the 21st day that is so obvious, was obvious in the paperwork that was given you in the very beginning and you walk away from this deal, you're not going to be able to get future deals. Only provide LOIs and start due diligence on deals you want to buy. Listen to what I'm telling you. Only provide LOIs and start due diligence on deals that you want to buy. Don't do this on deals you're like, I'm going to renegotiate this deal and play games with these people later on and maybe, maybe not depends on what I find and go into it with certainty. Otherwise you'll lose the most valuable thing that you have and that's your reputation as a closer. If you can't do that, if you can't do proper due diligence, you're well positioned. Once you can do proper due diligence and look for the balance between reasons to buy and reasons not to because you're going to find problems with the property, you'll be well positioned for success and have yourself in a situation where you can avoid the common pitfalls and budget suckouts that countless real estate investors, myself included, have had to deal with along the way. Look at the end of the day. Overpaying or taking on too much debt can sink you as can overwhelming misrepairs necessary to run the property properly. Unmanageable tenant basis, leases that are expiring and you didn't know about, leases that are actually bad leases, tenants that have the inability to even pay the rent. Don't let that be you that's going to not do a proper due diligence and don't let it be the guy or the gal that does due diligence and just mounts up every little tiny thing as a reason not to buy. Take reasonable steps before deciding if you want to do your deal. Bed bugs would not be a reason to walk from this deal. Every apartment building in this country has or will have bed bugs at some point. Trust me. Give your property due diligence. Balance that with the reasons to buy against the reasons not to buy. Think long term. Add your cash flow, your four quadrants. Look at your NOI.
